lesson.title: Genetics and Inheritance: Meiosis, Punnett Squares, Sex-Linked Traits, and Blood Groups (40 marks)
lesson.explanation: Meiosis is a type of cell division that produces four genetically unique haploid gametes (sex cells) from one diploid cell, halving the chromosome number. It involves two divisions (meiosis I separates homologous chromosomes; meiosis II separates chromatids) and introduces genetic variation through crossing over and independent assortment. Mendelian genetics uses Punnett squares to predict offspring ratios. Key concepts: dominant/recessive alleles, homozygous/heterozygous, genotype/phenotype. Incomplete dominance (e.g. snapdragons: red x white = pink) and co-dominance (e.g. ABO blood groups: IA and IB are co-dominant) extend Mendel's laws. Sex-linked traits are carried on the X chromosome (e.g. haemophilia, colour blindness) — males are more affected because they have only one X.

examples.problem: A heterozygous tall pea plant (Tt) is crossed with a homozygous short plant (tt). Draw a Punnett square and determine the phenotypic ratio of the offspring.

steps.instruction: Step 1: Parent genotypes — Tt (tall) x tt (short). Gametes: T and t from the tall parent; t and t from the short parent.
Step 2: Punnett square: T×t=Tt, T×t=Tt, t×t=tt, t×t=tt.
Step 3: Genotypic ratio: 2 Tt : 2 tt = 1 Tt : 1 tt. Phenotypic ratio: 2 tall : 2 short = 1 tall : 1 short.

examples.answer: Answer: Phenotypic ratio = 1 tall : 1 short (50% tall, 50% short). This is a test cross pattern.

examples.problem: Explain how the ABO blood group system demonstrates co-dominance and multiple alleles.

steps.instruction: Step 1: The ABO system has THREE alleles: IA, IB, and i. IA and IB are co-dominant (both expressed when present together); both are dominant over i (recessive).
Step 2: Genotypes and phenotypes: IAIA or IAi = Type A. IBIB or IBi = Type B. IAIB = Type AB (co-dominance — both A and B antigens expressed). ii = Type O.
Step 3: Example cross: Type A (IAi) x Type B (IBi). Punnett square: IAIB (AB), IAi (A), IBi (B), ii (O). Ratio = 1 AB : 1 A : 1 B : 1 O.

examples.answer: Answer: ABO shows co-dominance (IAIB = Type AB, both antigens present) and multiple alleles (3 alleles: IA, IB, i). Cross IAi x IBi gives 1 AB : 1 A : 1 B : 1 O.

examples.problem: Explain why haemophilia is more common in males than females, using a genetic cross.

steps.instruction: Step 1: Haemophilia is X-linked recessive. The allele is carried on the X chromosome. Males have XY (only one X), females have XX (two X chromosomes).
Step 2: A carrier female (XHXh) crossed with a normal male (XHY). Punnett square: XHXH (normal female), XHXh (carrier female), XHY (normal male), XhY (haemophiliac male).

Step 3: Males only need ONE copy of the recessive allele (Xh) to be affected because the Y chromosome has no corresponding allele. Females need TWO copies (XhXh) to be affected — they can be carriers (XHXh) without symptoms.

examples.answer: Answer: Males are more affected because they have only one X chromosome — one recessive allele (XhY) causes the disease. Females need two copies (XhXh), so they are usually carriers.

practice_problems.question: Define the following terms: allele, genotype, phenotype, homozygous, heterozygous.
practice_problems.answer: Allele: an alternative form of a gene (e.g. T for tall, t for short). Genotype: the genetic makeup (combination of alleles, e.g. Tt). Phenotype: the observable characteristic (e.g. tall). Homozygous: two identical alleles (TT or tt). Heterozygous: two different alleles (Tt).
practice_problems.explanation: These are foundational genetics terms. The genotype determines the phenotype: homozygous dominant (TT) and heterozygous (Tt) both show the dominant phenotype if dominance is complete.
distractors: Genotype is the physical appearance of an organism
Heterozygous means having two identical alleles
An allele is the same as a gene

practice_problems.question: In guinea pigs, black fur (B) is dominant over white fur (b). Two heterozygous black guinea pigs are crossed. Determine the expected genotypic and phenotypic ratios.
practice_problems.answer: Parents: Bb x Bb. Punnett square: BB, Bb, Bb, bb. Genotypic ratio: 1 BB : 2 Bb : 1 bb. Phenotypic ratio: 3 black : 1 white (75% black, 25% white).
hints: Draw the Punnett square with B and b gametes from each parent.
The classic 3:1 ratio comes from a monohybrid cross between two heterozygotes.
practice_problems.explanation: A monohybrid cross (Bb x Bb) gives the classic Mendelian 3:1 phenotypic ratio. The 1:2:1 genotypic ratio (BB:Bb:bb) underlies this.
distractors: Phenotypic ratio is 1:1 (half black, half white)
All offspring are black because black is dominant

practice_problems.question: In snapdragons, red flowers (RR) crossed with white flowers (WW) produce pink flowers (RW). This is incomplete dominance. If two pink snapdragons are crossed, what are the expected phenotypic ratios?
practice_problems.answer: Parents: RW x RW. Punnett square: RR (red), RW (pink), RW (pink), WW (white). Phenotypic ratio: 1 red : 2 pink : 1 white. This differs from complete dominance because the heterozygote shows a blended/intermediate phenotype.
hints: In incomplete dominance, the heterozygote is DIFFERENT from both homozygotes.
The genotypic and phenotypic ratios are the SAME (1:2:1) because each genotype has a unique phenotype.
practice_problems.explanation: Incomplete dominance: neither allele is fully dominant. RW = intermediate phenotype (pink). The 1:2:1 ratio differs from the 3:1 complete dominance ratio because heterozygotes are distinguishable.

practice_problems.question: A colour-blind man (XcY) marries a woman who is a carrier for colour blindness (XCXc). Determine the probability that their son will be colour-blind. [NSC-style question]
practice_problems.answer: Cross: XCXc x XcY. Punnett square: XCXc (carrier daughter), XCY (normal son), XcXc (colour-blind daughter), XcY (colour-blind son). Probability of a son being colour-blind: 1/2 of sons = 50%. Overall probability of any child being a colour-blind son: 1/4 = 25%.
hints: Write out the X and Y chromosomes with their alleles carefully.
Separate the question: probability of being male AND having the allele.
practice_problems.explanation: For sex-linked traits, track the X chromosomes carefully. Sons get their X from mother only (Y from father). If mother is XCXc, half her sons get Xc (colour-blind) and half get XC (normal).
distractors: No sons will be colour-blind because the father is affected

practice_problems.question: A paternity dispute arises in a South African court. The mother has blood type A (genotype IAi), and the child has blood type O (genotype ii). Man 1 has blood type AB (IAIB) and Man 2 has blood type B (IBi). Which man could be the father? Show your working using Punnett squares. [NSC-style 8-mark question]
practice_problems.answer: Man 1 (IAIB) x Mother (IAi): Possible offspring: IAIA (A), IAi (A), IAIB (AB), IBi (B). NO Type O possible. Man 1 CANNOT be the father. Man 2 (IBi) x Mother (IAi): Possible offspring: IAIB (AB), IAi (A), IBi (B), ii (O). Type O IS possible. Man 2 COULD be the father. The child's genotype ii requires one i from each parent. Man 1 (IAIB) has no i allele to contribute, ruling him out. Man 2 (IBi) has an i allele and could contribute it.
hints: The child is type O (ii) — each parent must contribute one 'i' allele.
Check whether each man has an 'i' allele to give.
practice_problems.explanation: ABO blood groups are used in paternity testing because the alleles follow strict inheritance rules. Type O (ii) requires both parents to carry at least one i allele. Man 1 (IAIB) has no i allele.
distractors: Man 1 could be the father because he has type A like the mother
Neither man could be the father
Both men could be the father

hints: Meiosis produces FOUR genetically DIFFERENT haploid cells (gametes). Mitosis produces TWO identical diploid cells.
Sex-linked traits: use XH and Xh notation. Males have only one X, so one recessive allele = affected.
ABO blood groups: IA and IB are CO-DOMINANT; both are DOMINANT over i. IAIB = type AB.

key_vocabulary.definition: Cell division producing four genetically unique haploid gametes from one diploid cell, involving two divisions and crossing over
key_vocabulary.example_usage: Meiosis in the testes produces sperm cells with 23 chromosomes each.

key_vocabulary.definition: The exchange of genetic material between homologous chromosomes during meiosis I, increasing genetic variation
key_vocabulary.example_usage: Crossing over produces recombinant chromosomes with new combinations of alleles.

key_vocabulary.definition: A pattern of inheritance where both alleles are fully expressed in the heterozygote
key_vocabulary.example_usage: In ABO blood groups, IAIB individuals have both A and B antigens (type AB blood).

key_vocabulary.definition: A trait determined by a gene located on the X chromosome; males are more commonly affected because they have only one X
key_vocabulary.example_usage: Colour blindness is sex-linked — about 8% of South African males are affected compared to <1% of females.

key_vocabulary.definition: A diagram used to predict the genotypic and phenotypic ratios of offspring from a genetic cross
key_vocabulary.example_usage: Using a Punnett square, we predict that Bb x Bb gives a 3:1 phenotypic ratio.

misconceptions.misconception: Students think dominant alleles are always more common in a population than recessive alleles.
misconceptions.correction: Dominant/recessive describes how alleles interact in a heterozygote, NOT their frequency. The recessive allele for blood type O (i) is actually the most common allele globally.
misconceptions.why_students_think_this: The word 'dominant' implies 'winning' or 'more powerful', suggesting it should be more common. Emphasise: dominance = expression pattern, not population frequency.

misconceptions.misconception: Students think sex-linked traits can be passed from father to son.
misconceptions.correction: X-linked traits pass from father to DAUGHTERS only (the father gives his X to daughters, Y to sons). A son gets his X chromosome from his MOTHER. A colour-blind father cannot have a colour-blind son unless the mother is at least a carrier.
misconceptions.why_students_think_this: Students think traits pass from father to son ('like father, like son'). Track the X and Y chromosomes carefully in every cross to see this is impossible for X-linked traits.

assessment.title: Assessment: Genetics and Inheritance (NSC exam format, 40 marks)

items.question: State two ways in which meiosis differs from mitosis.
items.correct_answer: 1) Meiosis produces 4 haploid cells; mitosis produces 2 diploid cells. 2) Meiosis involves crossing over and independent assortment (genetic variation); mitosis produces identical cells.
items.hint: Compare: number of divisions, number of daughter cells, ploidy, genetic variation.

items.question: Explain how crossing over during meiosis I increases genetic variation.
items.correct_answer: During meiosis I, homologous chromosomes pair up (synapsis). Non-sister chromatids exchange genetic material at points called chiasmata. This produces recombinant chromosomes with new combinations of alleles not found in either parent.
items.hint: What is exchanged? Between which structures?

items.question: A woman with blood type A (IAi) and a man with blood type B (IBi) have children. Draw a Punnett square and determine the possible blood types of their children.
items.correct_answer: IAIB (AB), IAi (A), IBi (B), ii (O). Possible blood types: A, B, AB, or O in a 1:1:1:1 ratio.
items.hint: Each parent contributes one allele. What combinations are possible?

items.question: Analyse why sex-linked recessive disorders like haemophilia affect males more frequently than females.
items.correct_answer: Males (XY) have only one X chromosome. One recessive allele on that X (XhY) is expressed because there is no second X to carry a dominant allele. Females (XX) need two recessive alleles (XhXh) to be affected; with one (XHXh), the dominant allele masks the recessive, making them carriers.
items.hint: Males have no 'backup' X chromosome to mask a recessive allele.

items.question: Evaluate whether it is possible for two parents with blood type A to have a child with blood type O. Justify your answer.
items.correct_answer: Yes, if both parents are heterozygous (IAi). Cross: IAi x IAi → IAIA (A), IAi (A), IAi (A), ii (O). There is a 25% chance of a type O child. Both parents carry the recessive i allele, and the child inherits i from each.
items.hint: Can type A parents carry a recessive i allele?

flashcards.front: How many cells does meiosis produce, and are they identical?
flashcards.back: FOUR genetically UNIQUE haploid cells (gametes). Compare: mitosis produces TWO identical diploid cells.
Exchange of genetic material between homologous chromosomes during meiosis I, producing recombinant chromosomes with new allele combinations.
Both alleles are fully expressed in the heterozygote. Example: IAIB = Type AB blood (both A and B antigens present).

flashcards.front: What are the ABO blood group genotypes?
flashcards.back: Type A: IAIA or IAi. Type B: IBIB or IBi. Type AB: IAIB. Type O: ii.

flashcards.front: Why are males more affected by sex-linked disorders?
flashcards.back: Males have only one X chromosome (XY). One recessive allele on X is expressed because the Y has no matching gene. Females (XX) need two copies to be affected.
The heterozygote shows an intermediate phenotype between two homozygotes. Example: red (RR) x white (WW) = pink (RW) in snapdragons.

flashcards.front: What phenotypic ratio does a monohybrid cross (Bb x Bb) produce?
flashcards.back: 3:1 (three dominant phenotype : one recessive phenotype). Genotypic ratio: 1 BB : 2 Bb : 1 bb.

flashcards.front: Can a father pass an X-linked trait to his son?
flashcards.back: No. Fathers give their Y chromosome to sons (and X to daughters). Sons inherit their X from their MOTHER.
